1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

9	/-!
10	# The `Scheme.Modules.tensorObj` substrate (A.1.c.SubT)
11	
12	This file is the **A.1.c.SubT** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the
13	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It records the dedicated
14	substrate on which the abelian-group instance of the relative Picard quotient
15	`Pic^♯_{C/k}(T) := Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` rests (the residual `sorry`
16	of `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean`).
17	
18	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
19	infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
20	`[L] + [L'] := [L ⊗ L']` on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three
21	ingredients on the Lean carrier:
22	
23	1. a binary tensor-product operation
24	   `⊗ : Scheme.Modules X × Scheme.Modules X → Scheme.Modules X`;
25	2. the structure sheaf `O_X` as a designated unit object for `⊗`;
26	3. an inverse operation on the full subcategory of invertible objects, i.e.
27	   the dual `L⁻¹ = Hom(L, O_X)` of an invertible sheaf.
28	
29	At Mathlib's pinned commit (`b80f227`), only a presheaf-level version of (1)
30	is available (`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`); (2) and (3) are present
31	as scheme-level objects, but the binary operation in (1) that ties them
32	together at the `Scheme.Modules` level is missing, and there is no
33	`MonoidalCategory` instance on `Scheme.Modules X`. This file records the
34	project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) + (3)
35	into a monoidal-category structure on `Scheme.Modules X`.
36	
37	## Status (iter-202 Lane TS — file-skeleton scaffold)
38	
39	This file is the **iter-202 Lane TS** file-skeleton: each of the 4 pinned
40	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
41	blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex`) with a
42	`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-203+ work: the `tensorObj` definition lifts
43	`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through sheafification, and the consumer
44	`PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj` then closes the residual `addCommGroup`
45	sorry of `RelPicFunctor.lean` L235.
46	
47	The 4 blueprint-pinned declarations are:
48	
49	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj` (def) — the substrate binary
50	   operation `⊗ : Scheme.Modules X × Scheme.Modules X → Scheme.Modules X`,
51	   lifting `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` on underlying presheaves
52	   composed with sheafification on the small Zariski site.
53	   Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`.
54	
55	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality` (def) — the
56	   functorial action of `⊗` on morphisms: a pair `f : M ⟶ M'`, `g : N ⟶ N'`
57	   determines `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`.
58	   Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`.
59	
60	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.monoidalCategory` (instance) — the
61	   monoidal-category structure on `Scheme.Modules X` with tensor `⊗`, unit
62	   `O_X`, associator, unitors, and braiding inherited from
63	   `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal` under sheafification.
64	   Per blueprint `thm:scheme_modules_monoidal`.
65	
66	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj` (def) — the
67	   `AddCommGroup` structure on the relative Picard quotient
68	   `Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT)`, built via the
69	   `tensorObj` substrate. This is the iter-204+ closure target for the Lane RPF
70	   L235/L266-269 `addCommGroup` residual sorry.
71	   Per blueprint `thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj`.
72	
73	Plus (PUSH-BEYOND) the supporting helper lemmas of the lift section
74	(`lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial`,
75	`lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible`, `lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct`,
76	`lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj`).
77	
78	## References
79	
80	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex` (740 LOC,
81	4 pins). Source: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
82	Defs. `df:aPf` + `df:Pfs`; Stacks tags 01CR (Picard group), 03DM (relative
83	tensor product of `O_X`-modules). Mathlib module
84	`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules`
85	(`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`).

95	
96	namespace Scheme

98	namespace Modules

100	/-! ## §1. The substrate tensor-product operation -/
101	
102	/-- **The substrate operation `⊗` on `Scheme.Modules X`.**
103	
104	For a scheme `X` and `M, N : X.Modules`, the tensor product
105	`M ⊗_X N : X.Modules` is the sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor
106	product `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` of the underlying presheaves of
107	`M` and `N` (affine-locally `(M ⊗_X N)(Spec A) = M(Spec A) ⊗_A N(Spec A)`).
108	
109	Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: the
110	body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body lifts
111	`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through the sheafification functor on
112	the small Zariski site of `X`. -/
113	noncomputable def tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
114	  sorry
115	
116	/-- **Functoriality of `⊗_X`.**
117	
118	A pair of morphisms `f : M ⟶ M'` and `g : N ⟶ N'` in `X.Modules` determines a
119	morphism `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`, compatible with identities
120	and composition; the assignment `(M, N) ↦ tensorObj M N` thereby extends to a
121	bifunctor `X.Modules × X.Modules ⥤ X.Modules` natural in both arguments.
122	
123	Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`. iter-202 Lane TS
124	scaffold: the body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body inherits the
125	morphism action from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` under
126	sheafification. -/
127	noncomputable def tensorObj_functoriality {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
128	    (f : M ⟶ M') (g : N ⟶ N') : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N' :=
129	  sorry
130	
131	/-! ## §2. The monoidal-category structure -/
132	
133	/-- **Monoidal-category structure on `Scheme.Modules X`.**
134	
135	The bifunctor `⊗_X` of `tensorObj_functoriality`, with unit object the
136	structure sheaf `O_X`, associator `α`, left/right unitors `λ`, `ρ`, and
137	braiding `β` inherited from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal` under sheafification,
138	satisfies the pentagon, triangle, and hexagon axioms; hence `Scheme.Modules X`
139	carries a canonical (symmetric) monoidal-category structure.
140	
141	Per blueprint `thm:scheme_modules_monoidal`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: the
142	body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body discharges the coherence axioms
143	affine-locally (each reduces to the analogous identity for the tensor product
144	of modules over a commutative ring) and assembles the
145	`CategoryTheory.MonoidalCategory` instance. -/
146	noncomputable instance monoidalCategory {X : Scheme.{u}} :
147	    MonoidalCategory (X.Modules) :=
148	  sorry
149	
150	/-! ## §3. The lift through `LineBundle.OnProduct` (PUSH-BEYOND supporting lemmas)
151	
152	The following helper lemmas record the lift of the substrate to the
153	locally-trivial subcategory used by the relative Picard consumer. They are not
154	`\lean{...}`-pinned in the blueprint (their statements are descriptive); the
155	typed signatures here scaffold the iter-203+ bodies. -/
156	
157	/-- **Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial.**
158	
159	If `M, N : X.Modules` are locally trivial of rank one (line bundles), so is
160	their tensor product `tensorObj M N`. Per blueprint
161	`lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: typed
162	`sorry`; the iter-203+ body trivialises both factors on a common affine cover
163	and uses that the tensor product of two free rank-one modules is free of rank
164	one. -/
165	lemma tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
166	    (hM : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial M) (hN : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial N) :
167	    LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial (tensorObj M N) :=
168	  sorry
169	
170	/-- **Inverse of an invertible module.**
171	
172	Every line bundle `L : X.Modules` has a two-sided tensor inverse: there is a
173	locally-trivial `Linv : X.Modules` (the dual `L⁻¹ = Hom(L, O_X)`) together with
174	a tensor isomorphism `L ⊗_X Linv ≅ 𝟙_ (X.Modules)`. Per blueprint
175	`lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: typed `sorry`;
176	the iter-203+ body builds the dual and the contraction isomorphism, which is an
177	isomorphism affine-locally on a trivialising cover. -/
178	lemma exists_tensorObj_inverse {X : Scheme.{u}} {L : X.Modules}
179	    (hL : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial L) :
180	    ∃ Linv : X.Modules, LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial Linv ∧
181	      Nonempty (tensorObj L Linv ≅ 𝟙_ (X.Modules)) :=
182	  sorry
183	
184	/-- **Restriction of `⊗` to the `LineBundle.OnProduct` carrier.**
185	
186	For `S`-schemes `C, T`, the bifunctor `⊗_{C ×_S T}` restricts to the subtype
187	`LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT` of locally-trivial modules on `C ×_S T`, with unit
188	the structure sheaf and the dual as two-sided inverse. Per blueprint
189	`lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: typed `sorry`. -/
190	noncomputable def tensorObjOnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S)
191	    (L L' : LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT) : LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT :=
192	  ⟨tensorObj L.carrier L'.carrier,
193	    tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial L.isLocallyTrivial L'.isLocallyTrivial⟩
194	
195	end Modules
196	
197	/-! ## §4. Consumer: the `AddCommGroup` instance on the relative Picard quotient -/
198	
199	namespace PicSharp
200	
201	/-- **Abelian-group structure on the relative Picard quotient via
202	`Scheme.Modules.tensorObj`.**
203	
204	For a base scheme `S`, a curve-side morphism `πC : C ⟶ S`, and a test object
205	`πT : T ⟶ S`, the relative Picard quotient
206	`Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT) = Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`
207	carries a canonical `AddCommGroup` structure with addition the descent of the
208	tensor product `[L] + [L'] := [L ⊗ L']` of `Scheme.Modules.tensorObj`, neutral
209	element `[O_{C ×_S T}]`, and inverse `-[L] := [L⁻¹]`.
210	
211	Per blueprint `thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj`. iter-202 Lane TS
212	scaffold: typed `sorry`. This is the iter-204+ closure target for the residual
213	`addCommGroup` sorry of `RelPicFunctor.lean` (L235); once this body lands, the
214	RPF instance closes against it. It is supplied as a `def` (rather than a global
215	`instance`) to avoid an instance diamond with the existing typed-`sorry`
216	`PicSharp.addCommGroup` instance in `RelPicFunctor.lean`. -/
217	noncomputable def addCommGroup_via_tensorObj {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
218	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) :
219	    AddCommGroup (Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT)) :=
220	  sorry
221	
222	end PicSharp
223	
224	end Scheme
225	
226	end AlgebraicGeometry

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-200). This chapter records the dedicated substrate
5	% on which the abelian-group instance of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
6	% (chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) rests. The genuine residual
7	% sorry at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean} L235 is
8	% the abelian-group law on the relative Picard quotient
9	% \(\Pic(C \times_k T)/\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\); descending tensor product of
10	% line bundles to that quotient requires a tensor-product operation on
11	% the underlying carrier and an inverse for invertibles, neither of
12	% which is presently exposed at the \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) level.
13	% Mathlib at the pinned commit ships
14	% \texttt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj} (a tensor product at
15	% the level of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\)), but does not lift this
16	% to a monoidal-category structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\). The
17	% chapter describes the substrate
18	% \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\), records the lift
19	% through the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) carrier used by
20	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, and pins the consumer
21	% instance back into \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The chapter is
22	% project-side and self-contained: dropping a Mathlib upstream PR is
23	% acceptable but downstream work does NOT wait for the PR to land.
24	
25	% NOTE (planner directive iter-200). The chapter has three
26	% responsibilities. First, it records what the Mathlib API surface
27	% currently exposes at the relevant abstraction levels, what is
28	% missing, and at which abstraction the project-side construction must
29	% land. Second, it spells out the construction of
30	% \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) as a binary operation
31	% on the category \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), its functoriality, and
32	% the monoidal-category axioms it has to satisfy in order for the
33	% downstream Picard-quotient consumer to work. Third, it spells out
34	% the canonical lift through the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)
35	% carrier and pins the consumer abelian-group instance on the relative
36	% Picard quotient back into chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
37	
38	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
39	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
40	
41	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
42	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
43	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
44	\[
45	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
46	  \qquad
47	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
48	\]
49	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
50	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
51	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
52	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
53	
54	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
55	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
56	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
57	% groups)
58	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
59	% SOURCE QUOTE:
60	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
61	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
62	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
63	% abelian groups defined by the formula
64	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
65	% \end{dfn}
66	%  [\dots]
67	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
68	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
69	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
70	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
71	% topologies  by
72	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
73	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
74	%  \end{dfn}"
75	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
76	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
77	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
78	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
79	homomorphisms.}
80	
81	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
82	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
83	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
84	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
85	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
86	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
87	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
88	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
89	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
90	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
91	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
92	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
93	structure. This is the content of
94	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
95	
96	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
97	infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
98	\[
99	  \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
100	  \;:=\; \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
101	\]
102	on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three ingredients on the
103	Lean carrier:
104	\begin{enumerate}
105	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
106	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
107	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\);
108	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
109	    for \(\otimes\);
110	  \item an inverse operation on the full subcategory of invertible objects,
111	    i.e.\ the dual \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L},
112	    \mathcal{O}_X)\) of an invertible sheaf.
113	\end{enumerate}
114	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
115	available; (2) and (3) are present as scheme-level objects, but the
116	binary operation in (1) that ties them together at the
117	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) level is missing. This chapter records the
118	project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) +
119	(3) into a monoidal-category structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
120	
121	\section{Mathlib API survey}
122	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
123	
124	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
125	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
126	
127	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
128	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
129	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
130	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
131	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
132	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
133	sectionwise tensor product
134	\[
135	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
136	\]
137	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
138	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
139	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
140	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
141	
142	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
143	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
144	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
145	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
146	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
147	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
148	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
149	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
150	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
151	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
152	tensor-product operation
153	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
154	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
155	
156	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
157	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
158	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
159	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
160	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
161	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) together with associator,
162	unitor, and braiding data. The project's policy on this gap is
163	\emph{project-side}: the substrate is built inside
164	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (a fresh file, sketched
165	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\), is the
166	natural home) and the consumer instance is closed against it. A
167	parallel Mathlib upstream PR with the same content is welcome but not
168	on the critical path for the project; the consumer is closed against
169	the project-side substrate independently of any upstream timing.
170	
171	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
172	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
173	
174	\begin{definition}
175	
176	\leanok
177	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
178	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
179	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
180	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
181	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
182	  \[
183	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
184	  \]
185	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
186	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
187	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
188	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
189	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
190	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
191	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
192	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
193	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
194	  canonical compatibility
195	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
196	     \;\cong\;
197	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
198	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
199	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
200	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
201	
202	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
203	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
204	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
205	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
206	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
207	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
208	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
209	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
210	\end{definition}
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	
214	\leanok
215	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
216	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
218	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
219	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
220	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
221	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
222	  \[
223	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
224	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
225	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
226	  \]
227	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
228	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
229	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
230	  identities and composition. The functor preserves the structure
231	  sheaf as a unit, in the sense that there are canonical natural
232	  isomorphisms
233	  \[
234	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
235	    \qquad
236	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
237	  \]
238	  and a canonical associator natural isomorphism
239	  \[
240	    \alpha_{M, N, P} \;\colon\;
241	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
242	      \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
243	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
244	  \]
245	  and a canonical braiding
246	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\), each
247	  inherited from the corresponding data on
248	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\) via the sheafification functor
249	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
250	\end{lemma}
251	
252	\begin{proof}
253	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
254	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
255	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
256	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial, has the ring as a
257	  monoidal unit, and is associative and symmetric up to canonical
258	  natural isomorphism. These data glue across the cover because the
259	  underlying gluing data of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect
260	  the canonical morphisms on overlaps. Equivalently, all four pieces of
261	  natural-isomorphism data (\(\lambda, \rho, \alpha, \beta\)) are
262	  inherited verbatim from the corresponding data on
263	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
264	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
265	  sheafification; the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site
266	  is symmetric monoidal because it preserves the relevant filtered
267	  colimits and finite limits used to construct the monoidal structure
268	  on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\).
269	\end{proof}
270	
271	\begin{theorem}
272	
273	\leanok
274	[Monoidal-category structure on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
275	  \label{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}
276	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.monoidalCategory}
277	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
278	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
279	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, with unit object
280	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), associator \(\alpha\), left unitor \(\lambda\),
281	  right unitor \(\rho\), and braiding \(\beta\) of
282	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, satisfies the
283	  pentagon, triangle, and hexagon axioms of a symmetric monoidal
284	  category. Consequently \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) carries a
285	  canonical symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure.
286	\end{theorem}
287	
288	\begin{proof}
289	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
290	  The pentagon and triangle identities (associator and unitor coherence)
291	  are inherited from the analogous identities on
292	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\). On any affine open
293	  \(\Spec A \subset X\), the assertions reduce to the analogous
294	  identities for the tensor product of \(A\)-modules, which are
295	  standard. The gluing data on overlaps is preserved by all four
296	  natural isomorphisms by construction, so the affine-level coherence
297	  identities yield global coherence identities on
298	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Symmetry of \(\beta\) follows by the
299	  same affine descent argument.
300	\end{proof}
301	
302	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
303	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
304	
305	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
306	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
307	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
308	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
309	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section spells out how the
310	monoidal-category structure of
311	\cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to that subtype, and how
312	the restriction interacts with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\).
313	
314	\begin{lemma}
315	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
316	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
317	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
318	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
319	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
320	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
321	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
322	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
323	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
324	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
325	\end{lemma}
326	
327	\begin{proof}
328	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
329	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
330	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
331	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
332	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
333	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
334	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
335	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
336	\end{proof}
337	
338	\begin{lemma}
339	[Inverse of an invertible module]
340	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
341	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
342	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
343	  bundle. The dual
344	  \[
345	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
346	  \]
347	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
348	  isomorphism
349	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
350	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
351	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
352	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
353	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
354	\end{lemma}
355	
356	\begin{proof}
357	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
358	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
359	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
360	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
361	  The contraction map
362	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
363	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
364	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
365	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
366	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
367	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
368	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
369	\end{proof}
370	
371	\begin{lemma}
372	[Restriction to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
373	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
374	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
375	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
376	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes and consider the
377	  \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier on \(C \times_S T\) of
378	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, concretised as the subtype
379	  \[
380	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
381	    \;\;=\;\;
382	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
383	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}.
384	  \]
385	  The bifunctor \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
386	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to a
387	  bifunctor on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with unit
388	  the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\), and the dual
389	  \(L \mapsto L^{-1}\) of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is a
390	  two-sided tensor inverse on this subcategory.
391	\end{lemma}
392	
393	\begin{proof}
394	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
395	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
396	  Direct from
397	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
398	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} applied to
399	  \(X := C \times_S T\): the tensor product of two locally-trivial
400	  rank-one modules is locally trivial of rank one, and the dual is
401	  again locally trivial of rank one. The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C
402	  \times_S T}\) is locally trivial of rank one (trivially), so it
403	  belongs to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). The
404	  monoidal-category structure of
405	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to the subcategory by
406	  the same affine descent argument.
407	\end{proof}
408	
409	\begin{lemma}
410	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
411	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
412	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
413	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
414	  functor
415	  \[
416	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
417	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
418	  \]
419	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
420	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
421	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
422	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
423	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
424	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
425	  canonical isomorphism
426	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
427	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
428	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
429	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
430	\end{lemma}
431	
432	\begin{proof}
433	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
434	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
435	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
436	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
437	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
438	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
439	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
440	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\) by the
441	  same descent argument as in
442	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}. The compatibility with the
443	  unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
444	  same cover.
445	\end{proof}
446	
447	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
448	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
449	
450	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
451	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
452	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
453	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
454	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
455	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
456	
457	\begin{theorem}
458	
459	\leanok
460	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
461	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
462	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
463	  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
464	        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
465	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
466	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
467	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
468	
469	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
470	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
471	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
472	  % groups)
473	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
474	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
475	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
476	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
477	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
478	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
479	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
480	  % \end{dfn}
481	  %  [\dots]
482	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
483	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
484	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
485	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
486	  % topologies  by
487	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
488	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
489	  %  \end{dfn}"
490	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
491	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
492	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
493	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
494	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
495	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
496	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
497	  \[
498	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
499	  \]
500	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
501	  abelian-group structure with addition
502	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
503	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
504	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
505	  inverse
506	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
507	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
508	  supplied by the substrate of
509	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricted to the
510	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier via
511	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
512	  map
513	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
514	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
515	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
516	\end{theorem}
517	
518	\begin{proof}
519	  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
520	        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
521	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
522	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
523	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
524	  By
525	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible},
526	  the substrate restricts to an abelian-group structure on the set
527	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T / \sim\) of isomorphism
528	  classes of line bundles on \(C \times_k T\). Concretely:
529	  associativity and commutativity come from the monoidal-category
530	  axioms of \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} (pentagon, triangle,
531	  hexagon coherence on isomorphism classes); the neutral element is
532	  the class of \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (left/right unitor
533	  coherence); and the inverse is the class of
534	  \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C
535	  \times_k T})\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}).
536	
537	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback
538	  functor
539	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
540	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) is a strong monoidal
541	  functor; in particular it carries products to products and preserves
542	  the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism classes
543	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
544	  homomorphism. Its image is therefore a subgroup of
545	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\).
546	
547	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set
548	  of \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of right cosets of this
549	  subgroup. The quotient by a subgroup of an abelian group is itself
550	  an abelian group, and the quotient map is a surjective group
551	  homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup --- the standard
552	  fact (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the
553	  general quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra
554	  textbook). Putting the pieces together gives the
555	  \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the
556	  statement, and identifies it with the structure of
557	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
558	\end{proof}
559	
560	\section{LOC estimates and prover-lane sequencing}
561	\label{sec:tensorobj_loc_estimates}
562	
563	The substrate splits into three logical pieces of roughly comparable
564	size; each is one prover lane in the iter-201+ schedule.
565	
566	\paragraph{Piece 1: definition + functoriality
567	(\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}).}
568	The Lean target file is the new
569	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}. The body

576	    presheaves and the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site;
577	  \item the functorial action on morphisms (bifunctoriality, identity

585	20$--$30$ LOC for bifunctoriality, $\approx 30$--$40$ LOC for the
586	natural-isomorphism data.

595	  \item assembling the data into a
596	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.MonoidalCategory} instance with target
597	    \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and into the symmetric variant
598	    (the hexagon for the braiding).
599	\end{itemize}
600	Estimate: approximately 60--100 LOC project-side, dominated by the
601	coherence-axiom discharge.

603	\paragraph{Piece 3: lift + consumer
604	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
605	       lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,

611	\(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup\_via\_tensorObj}\)
612	lives back in
613	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean}. The body
614	consists of:

617	    dual on a common trivialising cover (Piece 3a, $\approx 15$--$25$
618	    LOC);
619	  \item the dual is a two-sided tensor inverse (Piece 3b, $\approx
620	    15$--$25$ LOC);
621	  \item the bifunctor restricts to the
622	    \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (Piece 3c, $\approx 10$
623	    LOC);
624	  \item \(\pi_T^*\) is a strong monoidal functor (Piece 3d, $\approx
625	    10$--$20$ LOC);
626	  \item the consumer
627	    \texttt{addCommGroup\_via\_tensorObj} instance closing the
628	    file-local sorry of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
629	    encoding, via \texttt{QuotientAddGroup} on the descended subgroup
630	    of the abelian group built from Piece 1 + Piece 2 + Piece 3a--3d
631	    (Piece 3e, $\approx 10$--$20$ LOC).

635	\paragraph{Total + sequencing.} Roughly 200--320 LOC project-side, conservatively
636	budgeted as 200--400 LOC for the iter-201+ STRATEGY commitment. The

171	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
172	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
173	
174	\begin{definition}
175	
176	\leanok
177	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
178	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
179	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
180	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
181	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
182	  \[
183	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
184	  \]
185	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
186	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
187	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
188	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
189	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
190	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
191	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
192	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
193	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
194	  canonical compatibility
195	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
196	     \;\cong\;
197	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
198	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
199	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
200	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
201	
202	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
203	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
204	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
205	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
206	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
207	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
208	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
209	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
210	\end{definition}
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	
214	\leanok
215	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
216	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
218	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
219	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
220	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
221	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
222	  \[
223	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
224	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
225	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
226	  \]
227	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
228	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
229	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
230	  identities and composition. The functor preserves the structure
231	  sheaf as a unit, in the sense that there are canonical natural
232	  isomorphisms
233	  \[
234	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
235	    \qquad
236	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
237	  \]
238	  and a canonical associator natural isomorphism
239	  \[
240	    \alpha_{M, N, P} \;\colon\;
241	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
242	      \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
243	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
244	  \]
245	  and a canonical braiding
246	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\), each
247	  inherited from the corresponding data on
248	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\) via the sheafification functor
249	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
250	\end{lemma}
251	
252	\begin{proof}
253	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
254	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
255	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
256	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial, has the ring as a
257	  monoidal unit, and is associative and symmetric up to canonical

# AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean

## Summary
- Declarations added: **0** (axiom-clean).
- Declarations blocked: all of them — **environment blocker**, not a mathematical one.
- Sorry count: **unchanged** (file left exactly as the iter-202 scaffold; 6 stub sorries).
- The `.lean` file was **NOT modified** this iteration, deliberately (see "Why I stopped").

## Why I stopped — ENVIRONMENT BLOCKER (tool-result channel unusable)

This session's **tool-result channel is broken**: every tool result is being
truncated/dropped by the harness with

> "tool result was not added to context because it exceeded the configured max
> output limit of 8000 tokens."

This happens for **every** tool, including ones whose own output is essentially
empty:
- `Bash` running `echo hi` → dropped (>8000 tokens).
- `Read` of a 1-line `/tmp` file → dropped.
- `Glob` of `.claude/settings*.json` → dropped.
- An `Agent` subagent returning a <50-word reply → dropped.

The first ~8 tool calls of the session (the large PROGRESS.md / CLAUDE.md /
TensorObjSubstrate.lean reads) **succeeded**; the failure began afterward —
consistent with a large per-tool-result injection (MCP server tool catalogs
finishing connecting — `archon-lean-lsp`, `claude.ai Google Drive` — and/or a
large system-reminder/PostToolUse hook now appended to every result). The
appended block alone exceeds 8000 tokens, so **no amount of pagination/limits
on my own output recovers it** — confirmed by `echo hi` (zero meaningful output)
still overflowing.

### Impact on this lane (mathlib-build mode)
mathlib-build's core invariant is **verify each step axiom-clean before it
counts** (`lean_diagnostic_messages` / `lean_run_code` typecheck, then
`#print axioms`). With **zero readable tool output** I can:
- NOT search the Mathlib API (`lean_local_search`, `lean_loogle`, `leansearch`),
- NOT typecheck a draft signature,
- NOT see compiler errors,
- NOT run `lake build` or `#print axioms`.

Writing sheafification / monoidal-category code **blind** into a file that is
currently **GREEN** (verified-compiling scaffold) would risk turning it RED with
undetectable errors — strictly worse than the verified scaffold, and a violation
of the no-new-sorry / build-what-you-can-verify invariant. So I preserved the
file unchanged.

**This is `Blocked — environment, not mathematics`.** The COE-style escalation
pre-commitment does NOT apply to Lane TS (TS is substrate, not the 26-iter COE
sorry); but the planner should be aware this iteration produced no TS movement
purely because the tooling was unusable, and should **re-dispatch Lane TS
unchanged** once the harness tool-result channel is healthy.

## Diagnostic suggestions for the loop / next dispatch
1. **Check the tool-result size injection.** Inspect `.claude/settings.json` /
   `.mcp.json` for a PostToolUse hook or an MCP server whose tool catalog is
   injected into every result. The append is >8000 tokens, so it is almost
   certainly an auto-injected catalog/reminder, not normal command output.
2. If it is an MCP server (`claude.ai Google Drive` is the prime suspect for a
   huge tool catalog), consider disabling non-essential MCP servers for prover
   sessions — provers only need `archon-lean-lsp`.
3. Re-run the Lane TS dispatch verbatim afterward; the recipe below is ready to
   execute.

## Ready-to-execute recipe for next TS dispatch (unchanged from PROGRESS.md, with API-scouting notes)

Goal (Piece 1, PRIMARY): fill the body of
`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules`.

**First API scouting to do (could NOT be done this iter — channel broken):**
- Determine the exact definition of `Scheme.Modules X`. In recent Mathlib this
  is `SheafOfModules` over the structure sheaf of rings (the small Zariski
  site). Confirm with `lean_local_search "Scheme.Modules"` and inspect whether
  it is `SheafOfModules (X.ringCatSheaf)` / `SheafOfModules R` for some sheaf of
  rings `R`.
- **Check whether Mathlib ALREADY provides `MonoidalCategory (SheafOfModules R)`
  or `MonoidalCategory (Scheme.Modules X)`.** PROGRESS asserts it does not at
  commit `b80f227`, but verify directly:
  `lean_loogle "MonoidalCategory (SheafOfModules _)"` and
  `lean_loogle "MonoidalCategoryStruct (SheafOfModules _)"`. If such an instance
  exists, `tensorObj M N := M ⊗ N` (i.e. `MonoidalCategoryStruct.tensorObj M N`)
  closes Piece 1 immediately and most of the file collapses — **check this
  FIRST; it is the highest-leverage possibility.**
- Locate `PresheafOfModules.sheafification` (left adjoint to the
  sheaf→presheaf inclusion) and `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`. Confirm
  the namespace and exact signature; confirm the forgetful
  `SheafOfModules R ⥤ PresheafOfModules R.val` (`SheafOfModules.toPresheaf` /
  `.val`) and the sheafification functor on `SheafOfModules`.

**Construction (if no ready-made instance):**
`tensorObj M N := sheafify (M.val ⊗^pre N.val)`, i.e. apply
`PresheafOfModules.sheafification` to `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` of
the underlying presheaves `M.val`, `N.val`. The blueprint Layer 2/3
(`chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex` §"Construction strategy") specifies:
sheafify the presheaf tensor; transport the monoidal structure along the
sheafification⊣inclusion adjunction via
`CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Transport` (reflective-localization monoidal transport).

**Then** `tensorObj_functoriality` = sheafification-functor action on
`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`'s morphism map; **then** `monoidalCategory`
via `Monoidal.Transport` (do NOT hand-assemble pentagon/triangle — use the
transport lemma, which discharges coherence from the presheaf-level instance).

**CONTAMINATION GUARD (still in force):** `monoidalCategory` remains a `sorry`
instance; do not add any axiom-clean decl that *synthesizes*
`MonoidalCategory X.Modules` until that body is genuinely filled — otherwise a
`sorryAx`-tainted instance leaks into the typeclass cache.

## Per-declaration status (all NOT ADDED — environment-blocked, not attempted)
- `tensorObj` — NOT ADDED. Could not scout API or typecheck. Recipe above.
- `tensorObj_functoriality` — NOT ADDED. Depends on `tensorObj`.
- `monoidalCategory` (instance) — NOT ADDED. CONTAMINATION GUARD.
- `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial` — NOT ADDED.
- `exists_tensorObj_inverse` — NOT ADDED.
- `tensorObjOnProduct` — present (built on the stubs); unchanged.
- `PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj` — NOT ADDED (iter-204+ target).

## Verdict
`Blocked — environment, alternatives exhausted at the tooling level`:
the tool-result channel returned nothing readable for any tool (Bash/Read/Glob/
Grep/Agent all overflowed the 8000-token result cap due to a large per-result
injection). No Lean work could be verified, so per the mathlib-build no-unverified
invariant the file was left as the verified GREEN scaffold. Re-dispatch verbatim
once the harness tool-result channel is fixed.

{"file_path":"/home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf.lean","content":"/-
Copyright (c) 2024 Joël Riou. All rights reserved.
Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
Authors: Joël Riou
-/
module

public import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf
public import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Limits
public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.LocallyBijective
public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Whiskering

/-!
# Sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings

In this file, we define the category `SheafOfModules R` when `R : Sheaf J RingCat`
is a sheaf of rings on a category `C` equipped with a Grothendieck topology `J`.

-/

@[expose] public section

universe v v₁ u₁ u w

open CategoryTheory

variable {C : Type u₁} [Category.{v₁} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
  (R : Sheaf J RingCat.{u})

/-- A sheaf of modules is a presheaf of modules such that the underlying presheaf
of abelian groups is a sheaf. -/
structure SheafOfModules where
  /-- the underlying presheaf of modules of a sheaf of modules -/
  val : PresheafOfModules.{v} R.obj
  isSheaf : Presheaf.IsSheaf J val.presheaf

namespace SheafOfModules

variable {R}

/-- A morphism between sheaves of modules is a morphism between the underlying
presheaves of modules. -/
@[ext]
structure Hom (X Y : SheafOfModules.{v} R) where
  /-- a morphism between the underlying presheaves of modules -/
  val : X.val ⟶ Y.val

instance : Category (SheafOfModules.{v} R) where
  Hom := Hom
  id _ := ⟨𝟙 _⟩
  comp f g := ⟨f.val ≫ g.val⟩

@[ext]
lemma hom_ext {X Y : SheafOfModules.{v} R} {f g : X ⟶ Y} (h : f.val = g.val) : f = g :=
  Hom.ext h

@[simp]
lemma id_val (X : SheafOfModules.{v} R) : Hom.val (𝟙 X) = 𝟙 X.val := rfl

@[simp, reassoc]
lemma comp_val {X Y Z : SheafOfModules.{v} R} (f : X ⟶ Y) (g : Y ⟶ Z) :
    (f ≫ g).val = f.val ≫ g.val := rfl

variable (R)
/-- The forgetful functor `SheafOfModules.{v} R ⥤ PresheafOfModules R.val`. -/
@[simps]
def forget : SheafOfModules.{v} R ⥤ PresheafOfModules R.obj where
  obj F := F.val
  map φ := φ.val

/-- The forget functor `SheafOfModules R ⥤ PresheafOfModules R.val` is fully faithful. -/
@[simps]
def fullyFaithfulForget : (forget.{v} R).FullyFaithful where
  preimage φ := ⟨φ⟩

instance : (forget.{v} R).Faithful := (fullyFaithfulForget R).faithful

instance : (forget.{v} R).Full := (fullyFaithfulForget R).full

instance : (forget.{v} R).ReflectsIsomorphisms := (fullyFaithfulForget R).reflectsIsomorphisms

/-- Evaluation on an object `X` gives a functor
`SheafOfModules R ⥤ ModuleCat (R.val.obj X)`. -/
def evaluation (X : Cᵒᵖ) : SheafOfModules.{v} R ⥤ ModuleCat.{v} (R.obj.obj X) :=
  forget _ ⋙ PresheafOfModules.evaluation _ X

/-- The forget functor `SheafOfModules R ⥤ Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat`. -/
@[simps]
noncomputable def toSheaf : SheafOfModules.{v} R ⥤ Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{v} where
  obj M := ⟨_, M.isSheaf⟩
  map f := { hom := (forget R ⋙ PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R.obj).map f }

/--
The forgetful functor from sheaves of modules over sheaf of ring `R` to sheaves of `R(X)`-module
when `X` is initial.
-/
@[simps]
noncomputable def forgetToSheafModuleCat
      (X : Cᵒᵖ) (hX : Limits.IsInitial X) :
    SheafOfModules.{w} R ⥤ Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{w} (R.1.obj X)) where
  obj M := ⟨(PresheafOfModules.forgetToPresheafModuleCat X hX).obj M.1,
    Presheaf.isSheaf_of_isSheaf_comp _ _
      (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{w} (R.1.obj X)) AddCommGrpCat.{w}) M.isSheaf⟩
  map f := { hom := (PresheafOfModules.forgetToPresheafModuleCat X hX).map f.1 }

/-- The canonical isomorphism between
`SheafOfModules.toSheaf R ⋙ sheafToPresheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{v}`
and `SheafOfModules.forget R ⋙ PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R.val`. -/
noncomputable def toSheafCompSheafToPresheafIso :
    toSheaf R ⋙ sheafToPresheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{v} ≅
      forget R ⋙ PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R.obj := Iso.refl _

instance : (toSheaf.{v} R).Faithful :=
  Functor.Faithful.of_comp_iso (toSheafCompSheafToPresheafIso.{v} R)

instance (M N : SheafOfModules.{v} R) : AddCommGroup (M ⟶ N) :=
  (fullyFaithfulForget R).homEquiv.addCommGroup

@[simp]
lemma add_val {M N : SheafOfModules.{v} R} (f g : M ⟶ N) :
    (f + g).val = f.val + g.val := rfl

instance : Preadditive (SheafOfModules.{v} R) where
  add_comp := by intros; ext1; dsimp; simp only [Preadditive.add_comp]
  comp_add := by tauto

instance : (forget R).Additive where

instance : (toSheaf R).Additive where

variable {R}

/-- The type of sections of a sheaf of modules. -/
abbrev sections (M : SheafOfModules.{v} R) : Type _ := M.val.sections

/-- The map `M.sections → N.sections` induced by a morphism `M ⟶ N` of sheaves of modules. -/
abbrev sectionsMap {M N : SheafOfModules.{v} R} (f : M ⟶ N) (s : M.sections) : N.sections :=
  PresheafOfModules.sectionsMap f.val s

@[simp]
lemma sectionsMap_comp {M N P : SheafOfModules.{v} R} (f : M ⟶ N) (g : N ⟶ P) (s : M.sections) :
    sectionsMap (f ≫ g) s = sectionsMap g (sectionsMap f s) := rfl

@[simp]
lemma sectionsMap_id {M : SheafOfModules.{v} R} (s : M.sections) :
    sectionsMap (𝟙 M) s = s := rfl

variable (R) in
/-- The functor which sends a sheaf of modules to its type of sections. -/
@[simps]
def sectionsFunctor : SheafOfModules.{v} R ⥤ Type _ where
  obj M := M.sections
  map f := TypeCat.ofHom (sectionsMap f)

variable [J.HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat.{u} AddCommGrpCat.{u})]

variable (R) in
/-- The obvious free sheaf of modules of rank `1`. -/
@[simps]
noncomputable def unit : SheafOfModules R where
  val := PresheafOfModules.unit R.obj
  isSheaf := ((sheafCompose J (forget₂ RingCat.{u} AddCommGrpCat.{u})).obj R).property

/-- The bijection `(unit R ⟶ M) ≃ M.sections` for `M : SheafOfModules R`. -/
noncomputable def unitHomEquiv (M : SheafOfModules R) :
    (unit R ⟶ M) ≃ M.sections :=
  (fullyFaithfulForget R).homEquiv.trans M.val.unitHomEquiv

@[simp]
lemma unitHomEquiv_apply_coe (M : SheafOfModules R) (f : unit R ⟶ M) (X : Cᵒᵖ) :
    (M.unitHomEquiv f).val X = f.val.app X (1 : R.obj.obj X) := rfl

lemma unitHomEquiv_comp_apply {M N : SheafOfModules.{u} R}
    (f : unit R ⟶ M) (p : M ⟶ N) :
    N.unitHomEquiv (f ≫ p) = sectionsMap p (M.unitHomEquiv f) := rfl

lemma unitHomEquiv_symm_comp {M N : SheafOfModules.{u} R} (s : M.sections) (p : M ⟶ N) :
    M.unitHomEquiv.symm s ≫ p = N.unitHomEquiv.symm (sectionsMap p s) :=
  N.unitHomEquiv.injective (by simp [unitHomEquiv_comp_apply])

end SheafOfModules

namespace PresheafOfModules

variable (J)
variable {R : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}} {M₁ M₂ : PresheafOfModules.{v} R} (f : M₁ ⟶ M₂)

/-- A morphism of presheaves of modules is locally surjective
if the underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups is. -/
abbrev IsLocallySurjective : Prop :=
  Presheaf.IsLocallySurjective J ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R).map f)

/-- A morphism of presheaves of modules is locally injective
if the underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups is. -/
abbrev IsLocallyInjective : Prop :=
  Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective J ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R).map f)

variable {N : PresheafOfModules.{v} R} (hN : Presheaf.IsSheaf J N.presheaf)
  [J.WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat.{v}]
  [IsLocallySurjective J f] [IsLocallyInjective J f]

variable {J}

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
/-- The bijection `(M₂ ⟶ N) ≃ (M₁ ⟶ N)` induced by a locally bijective morphism
`f : M₁ ⟶ M₂` of presheaves of modules, when `N` is a sheaf. -/
@[simps]
noncomputable def homEquivOfIsLocallyBijective : (M₂ ⟶ N) ≃ (M₁ ⟶ N) where
  toFun φ := f ≫ φ
  invFun ψ := homMk (((J.W_of_isLocallyBijective
      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R).map f)).homEquiv _ hN).symm
      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R).map ψ)) (by
        obtain ⟨φ, hφ⟩ := ((J.W_of_isLocallyBijective
          ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R).map f)).homEquiv _ hN).surjective
          ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R).map ψ)
        simp only [← hφ, Equiv.symm_apply_apply]
        replace hφ : ∀ (Z : Cᵒᵖ) (x : M₁.obj Z), φ.app Z (f.app Z x) = ψ.app Z x :=
          fun Z x ↦ CategoryTheory.congr_fun (congr_app hφ Z) x
        intro X r y
        apply hN.isSeparated _ _
          (Presheaf.imageSieve_mem J ((toPresheaf R).map f) y)
        rintro Y p ⟨x : M₁.obj _, hx : f.app _ x = M₂.map p.op y⟩
        have hφ' : ∀ (z : M₂.obj X), φ.app _ (M₂.map p.op z) =
            N.map p.op (φ.app _ z) := CategoryTheory.congr_fun (φ.naturality p.op)
        change N.map p.op (φ.app X (r • y)) = N.map p.op (r • φ.app X y)
        rw [← hφ', M₂.map_smul, ← hx, ← (f.app _).hom.map_smul, hφ, (ψ.app _).hom.map_smul,
          ← hφ, hx, N.map_smul, hφ'])
  left_inv φ := (toPresheaf _).map_injective
    (((J.W_of_isLocallyBijective
      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R).map f)).homEquiv _ hN).left_inv
      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R).map φ))
  right_inv ψ := (toPresheaf _).map_injective
    (((J.W_of_isLocallyBijective
      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R).map f)).homEquiv _ hN).right_inv
      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R).map ψ))

end PresheafOfModules
"}

noncomputable def tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
      (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf) M.val N.val) :
    SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)

-- probe: is there a MonoidalCategory on the presheaf category, and what is tensorHom?
example {X : Scheme.{u}} (M M' N N' : X.Modules) (f : M ⟶ M') (g : N ⟶ N') :
    PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf) M.val N.val ⟶
      PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf) M'.val N'.val :=
  MonoidalCategory.tensorHom (C := PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
    f.val g.val

88	/-! ## §1. Line bundles on a product
89	
90	A *line bundle* on the relative curve `C ×_S T` is an invertible
91	`O_(C ×_S T)`-module, i.e. a sheaf of modules locally free of rank one. In
92	Mathlib `b80f227` the `Scheme.Modules` category is in place
93	(`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`), but there is no `IsInvertible`
94	predicate on its objects yet (the closest is `Module.Invertible R M` for rings,
95	in `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`). iter-187 supplies the missing
96	predicate as a project-side definition via local trivialisation on an affine
97	open cover (see `IsLocallyTrivial` below), and refines `OnProduct` to the
98	corresponding subtype.
99	
100	Blueprint reference: `def:line_bundle_on_product` (Kleiman §2, "absolute Picard
101	functor"; Stacks tag 01HK). -/
102	
103	/-- A sheaf of `𝒪_X`-modules `M` is **locally trivial of rank one** if every
104	point `x : X` has an affine open neighbourhood `U` on which the restriction
105	`M|_U` is isomorphic to the structure sheaf-as-module `𝒪_U`. This is the
106	project-side stand-in for the missing Mathlib `IsInvertible` predicate on
107	`Scheme.Modules`: line bundles (invertible sheaves) are exactly the
108	`IsLocallyTrivial` modules, by Stacks tag 01HK / Hartshorne II §6. Local
109	trivialisability is the structural content that survives base change along
110	`f : Y ⟶ X` (Stacks 01HH) and is the source of the invertibility-preservation
111	step for `pullbackAlongProjection`.
112	
113	The witness is bundled as `Nonempty (M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit _)`:
114	the existence of a sheaf-of-modules isomorphism over the affine chart. -/
